blurb: A low, spreading woodland native that floats a haze of sweetly fragrant lavender-blue flowers over the leaf litter each spring — one of the loveliest carpets for dappled shade and a magnet for early butterflies.
water: Moderate; keep the humusy soil evenly moist — not for hot dry sites
spread: Spreads slowly by decumbent rooting stems into loose mats; well-behaved
seasons: Fragrant lavender-blue flowers April–May; low leafy mats through summer in shade; semi-evergreen where mild, dying back in cold
wildlife: Nectar for early butterflies (swallowtails, skippers), long-tongued bees and hummingbirds; a valued spring nectar source
deer: Somewhat deer-tolerant; rabbits may browse the foliage
toxic: No known toxicity; grown as an ornamental, not a food plant
origin: Eastern and central North America (southern Ontario to Minnesota, south to Georgia and Louisiana)
habitat: Moist, humus-rich deciduous woodland, wooded slopes and shaded stream banks

care.hardiness: Hardy in USDA zones 3–8, so it winters easily in Boulder's 5b–6a climate; the real Front-Range challenge is not cold but our heat, sun and alkaline clay.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring after the mid-May frost or in early fall, in dappled to full shade with moist, humus-rich soil. This is a woodland plant, so give it the shadiest, most sheltered spot you have — the north or east side of the house, under deciduous trees, or among shrubs. In pots, use a rich, moisture-retentive mix and keep it out of afternoon sun.[1][2]
care.sun: Part shade to full shade — it takes dappled sunlight through a canopy but scorches and fades in hot, direct Front-Range sun; morning sun with afternoon shade is the sunniest it should get here.[2][1]
care.soil: Wants a humusy, organically rich, evenly moist but well-drained soil on the acidic-to-neutral side. Boulder's lean, alkaline clay is the opposite of its native leaf-mold woodland floor, so amend generously with compost and leaf mold and mulch to hold moisture and gently lower pH.[2][1]
care.water: Moderate and steady — keep the humusy soil evenly moist, especially through spring bloom and summer heat; it tolerates some drought and dry shade once established but is emphatically not a xeric plant for a dry, sunny bed.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space plants about 12–18 in. apart; the rooting stems knit into a loose groundcover mat over time.[2]
care.propagation: Easiest by division in early spring or right after bloom, or by layering — the trailing stems root where they touch moist soil, so rooted pieces lift and move readily. Stem cuttings taken after flowering root well, and it also grows from seed.[1][2]
care.bloom: April to May on the Front Range: loose clusters of five-petaled, slightly notched, sweetly fragrant flowers in lavender-blue, rose and violet over the low foliage.[1]
care.feeding: Topdress with compost or leaf mold in spring; it is not a heavy feeder and appreciates organic matter more than fertilizer.[2]
care.maintenance: Shear or cut the flowering stems back by about half right after bloom — this tidies the sprawling stems, encourages fresh basal foliage, and is the main defense against powdery mildew. Mulch to keep roots cool and moist through summer.[1][2]
care.selfsow: Spreads gently on its own by the decumbent stems rooting as they run, and self-sows modestly in congenial moist shade; it forms a loose colony rather than an aggressive one.[1]
care.troubles: Powdery mildew is the serious problem — it disfigures the foliage in warm, still, humid or drought-stressed conditions; improve air circulation, keep the soil evenly moist, and cut plants back after flowering. Spider mites appear in hot, dry spells and rabbits may nibble the foliage.[1][2]

edible.summary: Woodland phlox is grown as an ornamental, not a food — there is no documented, reliably-safe edible use, so keep it off the plate.[2]
edible.caution: No horticultural authority documents woodland phlox as a food plant; while phlox flowers are occasionally listed as an edible garnish, that use is not confirmed by a reliable safety source, so treat the plant as inedible. It is not noted as notably poisonous, but do not eat any part.[2]
